title: “Contemporary” Culture Epochs
caption: Table II from The Decline of the West, Vol. I. The art-history table: read a row across to see the architecture, sculpture, painting and music Spengler holds to be 'contemporary' — at the same stage of their Culture's style-cycle. The macro-phases are Pre-Cultural, the Culture's Early and Late periods (its 'great style'), and the formless Civilization.
source: Decline of the West, Vol. I — Table II, “Contemporary” Culture Epochs (foldout at end of volume).

rows.label: Chaos of primitive expression-forms. Mystical symbolism and naïve imitation.

apollinian.text: Mycenean Age (1600–1700). Fore-cultures: Late-Egyptian (Minoan), Late-Babylonian (Asia Minor).
apollinian.note: “(1600–1700)” as printed in the original edition (confirmed against Project Gutenberg #72344) — inconsistent with Table III, which dates the Mycenean Age 1600–1100 (the historical range); transcribed as printed.

rows.label: Early Period — ornament and architecture as the elementary expression of the young world-feeling (the “Primitives”).

magian.text: Early-Arabian form-world (0–500): Sassanid, Byzantine, Armenian, Syrian, Sabæan, “Late-Classical” and “Early Christian”.

rows.label: Birth and rise. Forms sprung from the land, unconsciously shaped.

egyptian.text: Dynasties IV–V (2930–2625). Geometrical temple-style. Pyramid temples. Ranked plant-columns. Rows of flat-relief. Tomb statues.

apollinian.text: 11th to 9th Centuries. Timber building. Doric column. Architrave. Geometric (Dipylon) style. Burial urns.

magian.text: 1st to 3rd Centuries. Cult interiors. Basilica, cupola (Pantheon as mosque). Column-and-arch. Stem-tracery filling blanks. Sarcophagus.

faustian.text: 11th to 13th Centuries. Romanesque and Early-Gothic vaulted cathedrals. Flying buttress. Glass-painting; cathedral sculpture.

rows.label: Completion of the early form-language. Exhaustion of possibilities. Contradiction.

egyptian.text: VI Dynasty (2625–2574). Extinction of the pyramid-style and the epic-idyllic relief style. Floraison of archaic portrait-plastic painting.

apollinian.text: 8th and 7th Centuries. End of the archaic Doric-Etruscan style. Proto-Corinthian–Early-Attic (mythological) vase.

magian.text: 4th–5th Centuries. End of Syrian, Persian and Coptic pictorial art. Rise of mosaic-picturing and of arabesque.

faustian.text: 14th–15th Centuries. Late Gothic and Renaissance. Floraison and waning of fresco and statue, from Giotto (Gothic) to Michelangelo (Baroque); Siena, Nürnberg; the Gothic picture from Van Eyck to Holbein. Counterpoint and oil-painting.

rows.label: Late Period — a group of urban, conscious arts in the hands of individuals (the “Great Masters”).

egyptian.text: XIth Dynasty. Delicate and telling art (almost no traces left).

apollinian.text: Completion of the temple-body (peripteros, in stone). The Ionic column. Reign of fresco-painting till Polygnotus (460). Rise of free plastic “in the round” (“Apollo of Tenea” to Hageladas).

magian.text: Completion of the mosque-interior (central dome of Hagia Sophia). Zenith of mosaic painting. Completion of the carpet-like arabesque style (Machatta).

faustian.text: The pictorial style in architecture, from Michelangelo to Bernini (d. 1680). Reign of oil-painting from Titian to Rembrandt (d. 1664). Rise of music from Orlando Lasso to H. Schütz (d. 1671).

egyptian.text: XIIth Dynasty (2000–1788). Pylon-temple, labyrinth. Character-statuary and historical reliefs.

apollinian.text: Maturity of Athens (480–350). The Acropolis. Reign of Classical plastic from Myron to Phidias. End of strict fresco and ceramic painting (Zeuxis).

magian.text: Ommayads (7th–8th Century). Complete victory of the featureless arabesque over architecture also.

faustian.text: Rococo. Musical architecture (“Rococo”). Reign of Classical music from Bach to Mozart. End of Classical oil-painting (Watteau to Goya).

rows.label: Exhaustion of strict creativeness. Dissolution of the grand form. End of the Style: “Classicism” and “Romanticism”.

apollinian.text: The age of Alexander. The Corinthian column. Lysippus and Apelles.

faustian.text: Empire and Biedermeyer. Classicist taste in architecture. Beethoven, Delacroix.

rows.label: “Modern art.” “Art-problems.” Attempts to portray or excite the megalopolitan consciousness; music, architecture and painting turn into mere craft-arts.

egyptian.text: Hyksos Period (preserved only in Crete; Minoan art).

apollinian.text: Hellenism. Pergamene art (theatricality). Hellenistic painting modes (veristic, bizarre, subjective). Architectural display in the cities of the Diadochi.

magian.text: Sultan dynasties of the 9th–10th Century. Prime of Spanish-Sicilian art. Samarra.

faustian.text: 19th and 20th Centuries. Liszt, Berlioz, Wagner. Impressionism from Constable to Leibl and Manet. American architecture.

rows.label: End of form-development. Meaningless, empty, pretentious architecture and ornament. Imitation of archaic and exotic motives.

egyptian.text: XVIII Dynasty (1580–1350). Rock-temple of Dehr-el-Bahri. Memnon-colossi. Art of Cnossos and Amarna.

apollinian.text: Roman Period (100–0–100). Indiscriminate piling of all three orders. Fora, theatres (Colosseum). Triumphal arches.

magian.text: Seljuks (from 1050). “Oriental art” of the Crusade period.

faustian.note: Spengler leaves the Western cell as a forward projection — this Civilization-phase lies in the West's future.

rows.label: Finale. A fixed stock of forms. Imperial display by means of material and mass. Provincial craft-art.

egyptian.text: XIX Dynasty (1350–1205). Gigantic buildings of Luxor, Karnak and Abydos. Small-art (beast-plastic, textiles, arms).

apollinian.text: Trajan to Aurelian. Gigantic fora, thermæ, colonnades, triumphal arches. Roman provincial art (ceramic, statuary, arms).

magian.text: Mongol Period (from 1250). Gigantic buildings (e.g. in India). Oriental craft-art (rugs, arms, implements).

faustian.note: As above, Spengler leaves the Western cell as a forward projection.
